title: Basic GET endpoint
prompt: Write a Spring Boot REST controller with a GET endpoint `/api/hello` that returns the plain text "Hello, Spring Boot!".
explanation: `@RestController` combines `@Controller` and `@ResponseBody`, so returned values are written directly to the HTTP response body (as plain text or JSON) rather than resolved as a view name.

title: GET endpoint with a path variable
prompt: Write a GET endpoint `/api/users/{id}` that returns the string "User: <id>" using the path variable.
explanation: `{id}` in the mapping path is a URI template variable, and `@PathVariable` binds it to the method parameter by matching name (or explicit `@PathVariable("id")` if the names differ).

title: POST endpoint accepting JSON
prompt: Write a POST endpoint `/api/products` that accepts a JSON body mapping to a `Product` record (`name`, `price`) and returns it back with HTTP 201.
explanation: `@RequestBody` tells Spring to deserialize the incoming JSON payload into the `Product` object using Jackson. Wrapping the return in `ResponseEntity` lets you control the exact HTTP status code (201 Created) instead of the default 200.

title: Configure the server port
prompt: Write the `application.properties` entry to make a Spring Boot app run on port 9090 instead of the default 8080.

correctAnswer.solution: server.port=9090

explanation: Spring Boot reads well-known configuration keys like `server.port` from `application.properties` (or `application.yml`) at startup to override embedded Tomcat's default port.

title: Inject a service into a controller
prompt: Write a `ProductService` bean and a controller that injects it via constructor injection to expose a GET `/api/products` endpoint returning `service.findAll()`.
explanation: `@Service` registers `ProductService` as a Spring-managed bean. Constructor injection (a single constructor, no `@Autowired` needed since Spring 4.3+) is the recommended DI style because it makes dependencies explicit and the class easy to unit test without a Spring context.

title: Basic repository interface
prompt: Given a `Product` JPA entity, write a Spring Data JPA repository interface that provides CRUD operations without any implementation code.

correctAnswer.solution:
public interface ProductRepository extends JpaRepository<Product, Long> {
}

explanation: Extending `JpaRepository<Product, Long>` (entity type, ID type) automatically provides `save`, `findById`, `findAll`, `deleteById`, and more at runtime via a Spring-generated proxy — no manual implementation is written.

title: Validate a request body
prompt: Add validation to a `CreateUserRequest` DTO so `email` must be non-blank and a valid email format, and wire up the controller to reject invalid requests with 400.
explanation: `@NotBlank`/`@Email` are Bean Validation annotations on the DTO fields; adding `@Valid` before `@RequestBody` in the controller tells Spring to run those validations automatically and, on failure, throw a `MethodArgumentNotValidException` that Spring's default handler converts into an HTTP 400 response.

title: Global exception handler
prompt: Write a `@RestControllerAdvice` that catches a custom `ProductNotFoundException` anywhere in the app and returns a JSON error body with HTTP 404.
explanation: `@RestControllerAdvice` applies exception handling globally across all controllers rather than duplicating try/catch logic in each one. `@ExceptionHandler` maps a specific exception type to a handler method that builds the actual error response.

title: Derived query method
prompt: Write a Spring Data JPA repository method that finds all products with `price` less than a given value, without writing any JPQL.
explanation: Spring Data JPA parses the method name (`findBy` + `Price` + `LessThan`) and generates the corresponding query automatically at startup — a "derived query method" — so no `@Query` annotation or manual JPQL is needed for simple conditions like this.

title: Transactional service method
prompt: Write a `transferFunds(Long fromId, Long toId, double amount)` service method that debits one account and credits another, ensuring both updates succeed or both roll back together.
explanation: `@Transactional` wraps the whole method in a single database transaction; if any exception is thrown partway through (e.g. the second `findById` fails), Spring automatically rolls back both changes so the accounts are never left in an inconsistent half-updated state.

title: Type-safe external configuration
prompt: Write a `@ConfigurationProperties` class that binds a `mail.host` and `mail.port` property from `application.properties` into typed fields.
explanation: `@ConfigurationProperties(prefix = "mail")` binds any `mail.*` property (e.g. `mail.host=smtp.example.com`) to a matching setter, giving compile-time-safe, typed access to configuration instead of scattering `@Value("${mail.host}")` across multiple classes.

title: Slice test for a REST controller
prompt: Write a `@WebMvcTest` for `ProductController` that mocks `ProductService` and asserts a GET `/api/products/1` returns HTTP 200 with the expected JSON.
explanation: `@WebMvcTest` loads only the web layer (controllers, filters, JSON mapping) instead of the full application context, which is much faster than `@SpringBootTest`. `@MockBean` replaces the real `ProductService` with a Mockito mock so the test can control its behavior in isolation, and `MockMvc` simulates the HTTP request without starting a real server.

title: Secure an endpoint by role
prompt: Write the Spring Security configuration and method-level annotation needed so that only users with role `ADMIN` can call `DELETE /api/products/{id}`.
explanation: The `SecurityFilterChain` rule enforces the role at the URL/HTTP-method level as the first line of defense, while `@PreAuthorize` (enabled by `@EnableMethodSecurity`) adds a second, defense-in-depth check directly on the method — useful because it's still enforced even if the method is later called from somewhere the URL-based rule doesn't cover, like an internal service call.

title: Circuit breaker around an external call
prompt: Write a service method that calls an external payment API, wrapped with a Resilience4j circuit breaker that falls back to a queued-for-retry response if the circuit is open.
explanation: `@CircuitBreaker` (Resilience4j's Spring Boot integration) tracks the call's failure rate against `paymentApi`'s configured thresholds; once the circuit trips open, subsequent calls skip the real `client.charge()` entirely and go straight to `fallbackMethod`, protecting the system from cascading failures when the payment provider is degraded.

title: Cache a lookup and evict it on update
prompt: Write a `ProductService` where `getProduct(id)` is cached, and `updateProduct(id, ...)` correctly evicts that specific cache entry so stale data is never served after an update.
explanation: `@Cacheable(key = "#id")` stores each product under a cache key derived from its id, avoiding a database hit on repeated reads. Critically, `@CacheEvict` on `updateProduct` uses the *same* key expression so the exact stale entry is removed on write — a mismatched or missing eviction here is one of the most common real-world Spring caching bugs, since reads would keep serving outdated data indefinitely.

title: GET endpoint with optional query param
prompt: Write a GET endpoint `/api/search` that accepts an optional `keyword` query parameter, defaulting to an empty string if not provided.
explanation: `@RequestParam(defaultValue = "")` makes the parameter optional and supplies a fallback value automatically if it's missing from the request, avoiding a manual null check in the method body.

title: Return different status codes based on a result
prompt: Write a GET `/api/products/{id}` endpoint that returns the product with HTTP 200 if found, or HTTP 404 with no body if not found.
explanation: Returning `ResponseEntity<T>` instead of a bare `T` lets the method control the exact HTTP status per outcome; `Optional.map`/`orElseGet` cleanly express the two branches without an explicit if/else.

title: Reduce boilerplate with Lombok
prompt: Write a `Product` class with `name` and `price` fields using Lombok annotations to auto-generate getters, setters, and a constructor with all fields.
explanation: Lombok annotations generate the getter/setter/constructor boilerplate at compile time via annotation processing, so the source file stays short while the compiled class still has the full set of methods a hand-written version would have.

title: Load different config per environment
prompt: Write the file naming convention for a Spring Boot properties file that should only apply when the `dev` profile is active, and the command-line flag to activate that profile.

correctAnswer.solution:
File: application-dev.properties (or application-dev.yml)
Activation: --spring.profiles.active=dev

explanation: Spring Boot automatically loads `application-<profile>.properties` in addition to the base `application.properties` when that profile is active, letting environment-specific overrides (dev/staging/prod) live in separate files instead of one giant conditional config.

title: Add a logger to a service class
prompt: Write a `ProductService` class with a logger that logs an info message "Fetching all products" at the start of a `findAll()` method.
explanation: `LoggerFactory.getLogger(ProductService.class)` (SLF4J, which Spring Boot wires to Logback by default) is the standard way to get a class-scoped logger; using `log.info()` instead of `System.out.println()` respects configured log levels and output destinations in production.

title: Expose a health check endpoint
prompt: Write the `application.properties` entries needed to expose Spring Boot Actuator's `/actuator/health` endpoint.

correctAnswer.solution:
management.endpoints.web.exposure.include=health
management.endpoint.health.show-details=always

explanation: Actuator endpoints are not all exposed by default for security reasons; `management.endpoints.web.exposure.include` explicitly whitelists which ones are reachable over HTTP, and `show-details=always` includes component-level health breakdowns (DB, disk space, etc.) rather than just a top-level UP/DOWN status.

title: Map an entity to a DTO manually
prompt: Given a `Product` JPA entity and a `ProductDto` (id, name, price only, no internal fields), write a method that converts one to the other.
explanation: Mapping entities to DTOs before returning them from a controller prevents internal persistence details (lazy-loaded associations, audit fields, JPA proxies) from leaking into the public API contract and being accidentally serialized.

title: Paginated list endpoint
prompt: Write a GET `/api/products` endpoint that returns a paginated list of products, accepting `page` and `size` query parameters via Spring's Pageable support.
explanation: Spring Web MVC automatically resolves a `Pageable` method parameter from `page`/`size`/`sort` query parameters without any manual parsing, and `JpaRepository.findAll(Pageable)` translates it directly into a `LIMIT`/`OFFSET` SQL query — critical for any endpoint that could otherwise return an unbounded result set.

title: Conditionally register a bean
prompt: Write a configuration class that registers a `FakePaymentGateway` bean only when the `dev` profile is active, and a `RealPaymentGateway` bean otherwise.
explanation: `@Profile` on a `@Bean` method restricts when Spring registers that bean; `@Profile("!dev")` means "any profile except dev", ensuring exactly one `PaymentGateway` bean exists regardless of which profile is active, avoiding an ambiguous-bean startup error.

title: Log every request's duration
prompt: Write a Spring `HandlerInterceptor` that logs how long each HTTP request took to process.
explanation: `preHandle` runs before the controller method and `afterCompletion` runs after the response is fully sent (including after any exception), so storing the start time as a request attribute and diffing it in `afterCompletion` correctly measures the full request lifecycle regardless of whether the request succeeded or failed.

title: Run a background job on a fixed schedule
prompt: Write a Spring Boot scheduled method that runs every 30 minutes to clean up expired sessions.
explanation: `@Scheduled(fixedRate = ...)` (milliseconds) runs the method repeatedly at that interval, measured from the start of one execution to the start of the next; `@EnableScheduling` must also be added to a `@Configuration` class for `@Scheduled` methods to actually run.

title: Add a custom Actuator health check
prompt: Write a custom Actuator HealthIndicator that reports DOWN if a critical external payment API is unreachable.
explanation: Implementing `HealthIndicator` and registering it as a `@Component` automatically adds it as a named section under `/actuator/health`, so infrastructure monitoring (load balancers, Kubernetes liveness probes) can see this specific dependency's status alongside the built-in DB/disk checks.

title: Complex query with JPQL and joins
prompt: Write a Spring Data JPA repository method using `@Query` that returns products whose category name matches a given string, joining through a `Category` entity.
explanation: `@Query` with JPQL is needed once the query requires a join through an entity relationship that a simple derived method name (`findByCategory_Name`, which would actually also work here) becomes awkward to express, or when the query needs custom joins, subqueries, or projections beyond what method-name derivation supports.

title: Cross-cutting logging with an aspect
prompt: Write a Spring AOP aspect that logs the execution time of every method in any class annotated `@Service`, without modifying those classes directly.
explanation: `@Around` advice with the pointcut `within(@Service *)` matches every method call on any class carrying the `@Service` annotation, and `joinPoint.proceed()` actually invokes the original method — this is how cross-cutting concerns like timing, logging, or auditing get applied uniformly without touching each service class's own code.

title: Make a POST endpoint safe to retry
prompt: Write a POST `/api/orders` endpoint that accepts an `Idempotency-Key` header and ensures that retrying the same request (same key) never creates a duplicate order.
explanation: Storing the client-supplied `Idempotency-Key` on the created order (with a unique DB constraint on that column as the real safety net against race conditions) lets a retried request with the same key return the original result instead of creating a second order — essential for any payment or order-creation endpoint where a network timeout might cause a client to retry a request that actually already succeeded server-side.
